title: Infants and young children
summary: Young children usually need supportive care rather than cough-and-cold medicines. Age-specific fever and breathing signs matter more than the exact label of the virus.
evidenceSnapshot: Fluids or feeds, saline nose drops and careful monitoring are the main home measures. Honey is unsafe under 12 months, and Australian regulators restrict sedating antihistamines for cough/cold/flu in children under 6.

interventions.name: Frequent breastfeeds, formula or usual fluids
interventions.finding: Helps prevent dehydration when intake is reduced.
interventions.practical: Offer smaller amounts more often; track wet nappies or urine.
interventions.cautions: Seek review if the child cannot keep fluids down or urinates much less.

interventions.name: Saline nose drops or spray
interventions.finding: Can loosen nasal mucus and make feeding or sleep more comfortable.
interventions.practical: Use a child-appropriate saline product before feeds or sleep; gently wipe or suction only if needed.
interventions.cautions: Avoid forceful suction, which can irritate the nose.

interventions.name: Paracetamol or ibuprofen for distress
interventions.finding: Can relieve fever-related discomfort and pain; the goal is comfort, not forcing temperature to normal.
interventions.practical: Dose by the child's current weight and the product label. Ibuprofen is generally for children older than 3 months unless a clinician advises otherwise.
interventions.cautions: Do not alternate routinely without professional advice; avoid ibuprofen with dehydration or certain medical conditions.

interventions.name: Honey for cough
interventions.finding: May reduce night cough in children older than 12 months.
interventions.practical: A small spoonful before bed can be tried from 12 months of age.
interventions.cautions: Never give honey to a baby under 12 months because of infant botulism risk.

interventions.name: OTC cough, cold and decongestant medicines
interventions.finding: There is little evidence of meaningful benefit in young children and a real risk of dosing errors and adverse effects.
interventions.practical: Do not use unless a doctor or pharmacist specifically recommends an age-appropriate product.
interventions.cautions: First-generation sedating antihistamines must not be used for cough/cold/flu in children under 6 in Australia; adult products are inappropriate.

practicalSteps: Keep the child close enough to notice work of breathing, colour, alertness and fluid intake.
Use saline before feeds if congestion is making sucking difficult.
Let the child rest, but use normal safe-sleep practices: on a flat, firm surface without pillows or improvised elevation for babies.
Write down medicine name, strength, dose and time to prevent accidental repeat dosing.
avoidOrCare: No aspirin in children unless specifically prescribed.
Do not use steam bowls, essential oils near the face, or adult vapour rubs in babies.
Avoid cold baths, stripping the child or trying to 'sweat out' a fever.

myths.claim: A high fever number alone tells you how sick a child is.
myths.reality: Age, breathing, alertness, hydration and the overall pattern are crucial; any fever in a baby under 3 months needs urgent assessment.

myths.claim: Cough syrup helps young children sleep and recover.
myths.reality: Benefit is unproven for most products, while sedation and dosing harms are well documented.

redFlags: Baby younger than 3 months with a temperature of 38°C or higher — urgent medical assessment
Blue, grey or very pale colour; grunting; ribs sucking in; pauses in breathing; severe wheeze or stridor
Very sleepy, difficult to wake, floppy, confused, inconsolable or having a seizure
Poor feeding with fewer wet nappies, no tears, dry mouth or sunken eyes
Symptoms worsening, or not starting to improve after about 48 hours in a young child
unsettled: Evidence for nasal suction devices is limited; gentle use may help selected infants, but repeated or forceful suction can worsen irritation.
